text: This is not an official document. The official documents exist and are filed correctly and CAMPBELL has indexed them and the Director has read them and they are fine as far as they go. They just don't go very far.

I am John Johnson. I work in the lab. I do not go in the field. I make tea and I run pattern analysis and I occasionally notice things that the people who are busy being competent in dangerous situations don't have time to notice. I write those things down here.

Methodology: I sent a standardised questionnaire to all four operatives after each case. Response rate: variable. I also asked questions in the kitchen, in the kind of conversation people have when they are eating toast and not paying attention to what they are saying. That is where I get the best data.
If anyone is reading this who shouldn't be: hello. I hope you find it useful. I hope you wonder why I left it somewhere you could find it.

blocks.heading: Intake Note — J. Johnson
text: CAMPBELL flagged this case twenty-three days before anyone read it. I want to be careful here because I am not accusing anyone of negligence. The queue is long. Low-priority items wait. That is the system working as designed.
What I am noting — just noting — is that between the flagging and the reading, three people were hurt. One of them had grey in his hair by the time they found him, and he was twenty years old.

The ash particulate. The cold. The handprint bruises shaped from the inside. CAMPBELL rated it low priority. CAMPBELL is very good at its job. I am not sure what to do with the gap between those two facts, so I am writing it down instead.

Rex Bangley received the Director's PDA messages directly. He did not share them with the team. He started sending field samples back to the lab immediately — swabs, photographs, the works — the way a surgeon sends a biopsy before the diagnosis is confirmed. I have always found Rex's relationship to data both admirable and slightly alarming. He was doing his job. He was not, so far as I can tell, troubled by the question of what the lab would do with what he found.
I am noting that too.

blocks.heading: Field Questionnaire — Case 01
blocks.subheading: Selected responses with editorial annotation — J. Johnson
blocks.question: Describe your first impression of the campus.

responses.name: Rex Bangley
responses.text: Standard mid-tier research institution. Ash particulate present at three confirmed sites. Disciplinary records cross-reference with a single student. Cold signatures consistent with Class 2 spectral activity. I wore the biohazard suit from Scene 1. In retrospect this was the correct call.
responses.annotation: Rex mentioned the hazmat suit unprompted. He wore it for the duration of Scene 1. I have thought about this a lot. On the one hand it is genuinely efficient field protocol for unknown particulate. On the other hand, a man in a biohazard suit swabbing a bulletin board while two colleagues were trapped in an administrative meeting approximately thirty meters away is a very specific image. I am choosing to interpret it as efficient.

responses.name: Reed Atwood
responses.text: The campus felt normal. That was the strangest part. Everything looked the way it should look. I kept expecting it to feel wrong and it just... didn't. Not until Rex called in about the cold spot.

responses.name: Sven
responses.text: There were a lot of people.
responses.annotation: Sven's questionnaire responses across both cases average approximately eight words per answer. I have stopped taking this personally.

responses.name: Alan Frazier
responses.text: Cold. Not the temperature — I mean the feeling. Like a room after someone has been crying in it and cleaned up and you can't see anything wrong but you know.

blocks.heading: Reconstructed Scene — The Security Office
blocks.subheading: From three separate accounts, none of which agree on the key details
text: The team split early. Rex and Alan were free to investigate. Reed and Sven were not.

What happened: the team attempted to persuade Campus Security Officer Oskar and Detective Vasquez to share case information beyond what was already public. The roll failed — I am told it was close, which I am choosing not to find comforting. The consequence was that Rex and Alan could work the campus unimpeded, while Reed and Sven had to stay in the security office and be cooperative.

Detective Vasquez, by all accounts, was thorough. She went through the suspect list methodically. She described each person of interest. She included, at some point in the middle of this, a first-year CS student named Bálint Kovács — quiet, isolated, no priors, barely leaves his dorm room.
Reed and Sven, I am told, were not paying close attention by this point.

blocks.text: I want to be precise about this. At the exact moment that Detective Vasquez was describing the boy at the centre of everything, the two operatives who were supposed to be listening were not listening. Reed has since told me they were waiting for Rex to call in. Sven has told me, in six words, that the meeting was long. I find both of these explanations completely understandable and also somehow very funny and also somehow very sad.

text: The Sven detail: at some point during the meeting, Sven considered walking through the wall. The accounts diverge here. Reed says Sven "seemed like he was thinking about it." Sven's questionnaire says "the wall was there." What I understand is that common sense prevailed — or rather, that the number of witnesses who would have seen a man pass through solid concrete was prohibitive. They stayed. They waited. They acquired a police tail for their trouble, who spent the rest of the case a careful distance behind them, increasingly confused.

Meanwhile: Rex Bangley, in a biohazard suit, was kneeling next to a campus bulletin board in a corridor that had gone cold, methodically swabbing grey ash from around a collection of notices. The notices were Bálint's. Notes from friends, a missing birthday card, a printed photo of a girl in Hungary. And the ash was everywhere around them — concentrated, deliberate, present in the specific shape of something protecting what it loved.
Rex was very thorough. He swabbed all of it. At some point, something noticed.

blocks.speaker: Reed Atwood, later, on the phone
blocks.text: Rex said he felt cold. He said it was the real kind. Not temperature — the other kind. He kept working anyway.

blocks.heading: The Infirmary — A Note on Alan's Methods
text: The team wanted to speak with Marcus Webb — the most recent victim, in the infirmary with grey hair he hadn't had before and bruises shaped like hands.
Marcus had a girlfriend. Alan Frazier used his glamour to become her.

I want to be clear that this is a completely reasonable use of available resources. Marcus was frightened and in pain and had no reason to talk to strangers. He had every reason to talk to someone he trusted. Alan gave him that. It worked.

What I find interesting is that Marcus spent most of the conversation trying to rationalise. He didn't want to use the word ghost. He built elaborate structures around what had happened to him: electrical fault, gas, something he ate. He knew. He had grey hair and handprint bruises and he had looked directly at her and he knew, and he still would not say it.
Alan nodded and let him talk.
I think about Marcus Webb more than the official record would suggest.

blocks.heading: The Locket — And What Happened To It
text: They found the locket in Bálint's room. On a folded cloth, on a desk that had gone cold, surrounded by ash that would not wash away. Reed took it.

The reports describe what followed as "escalating weight and temperature effects." This is accurate and also misses something. It was heavier. That's the plain fact. A small gold locket, the kind you could close in a fist, that weighed the way something weighs when it has been carried too long. Reed put it in his bag and walked and every step it was heavier and colder and he didn't put it down.

blocks.speaker: Reed Atwood
blocks.text: I don't know. It felt like if I put it down that would mean something. I didn't want it to mean something.

text: They found Bálint on the footbridge between the science and engineering blocks. They weren't looking for him there — they were chasing Amanda, trying to follow the thread of the last victim, and they were told the robotics lab was where she'd be. The footbridge was on the way. He was sitting on the railing in the way people sit when they are not in danger but want you to know they are very tired.
He saw them coming. He saw Reed's bag. He understood what was in it before Reed said a word.

Four accounts of this meeting and they all say the same thing: it was gentle. Nobody pushed. Nobody made demands. Reed gave back what wasn't his to take. Bálint held the locket and said: *She knows you have it. She has known this whole time. She is — she is frightened you will take it somewhere she cannot follow.*
They went to the lab together.

blocks.heading: The Robotics Lab — Reconstructed
text: The screen showed a cursor. Then it typed.

The accounts differ on exactly what she wrote — the letters came slowly, and everyone was watching different things. What everyone agrees on is that it was in Hungarian, and that it was addressed to Bálint, and that the word *bálintkám* appeared early and stayed.

blocks.text: Bálintkám. It is a diminutive. An endearment. "My little Bálint." It is the kind of word you use when someone is very young, or when you are afraid, or when you love them so much the ordinary word isn't enough.

text: Reed had the locket. He handed it to Bálint. This part was correct. What came next was — less correct. Reed decided that the safest resolution was to break the locket and burn what was inside. I believe he was trying to help. I believe this completely. I also believe that the intent of an action and its effect on the person it lands on are not the same thing, which is a lesson that case 01 teaches very clearly.
The locket broke. The ash hit the floor of the robotics lab in the blue light of standby monitors.
Eszter escalated.

The robots came online — four of them, dog-sized, circling — and the screen went dark and the temperature dropped and Bálint was on his knees on the floor with the broken locket and he was crying. He was nineteen years old and he was crying on the floor of a robotics lab while four machines that his girlfriend was operating closed in around him, and he kept saying something in Hungarian that nobody translated until later.

blocks.text: He was saying: I'm sorry. I'm sorry I couldn't keep them away. I'm sorry.

text: Rex stabbed an electrical cable into the router junction. He shorted the wireless. The robots lost signal and went still mid-step. I am told this was improvised. I am told the specific tool used was "whatever was closest." Rex Bangley, I want the record to reflect, is a remarkable human being.

Alan ran for the chemistry corridor. He found the police officer in the hallway — the one who had been following them all day, notebook out, increasingly bewildered. Alan and Reed came back with acid.
Sven stayed.

Sven found a battery-powered laptop in the corner of the lab — a student's, left on standby — and he brought it to Bálint, and he sat down on the floor next to him, and he said something quiet that nobody else heard. And Bálint wiped his face and opened the laptop.
He typed for a long time.
Alan dissolved the ash in acid.
The screen showed one word in response: *megyek.* I'm going.

The lab received partial ash samples from Rex's earlier swabbing. These have since been developed as a research thread — something about the way the particulate interfaces between entities and electronics. The work is ongoing. I check on it sometimes.
Bálint Kovács called the PORTAL contact number three times in the weeks after the case closed. Nobody answered.

I am noting this. I do not know whose job it was to answer. I do not know if it was anyone's job, formally. I am noting it anyway, because it seems like the kind of thing that should not simply not be noted.

The police officer who followed the team all day filed a report. I have read it. It describes, in careful bureaucratic language, an anomalous event at the Keller University engineering block involving electrical failure, controlled chemical dissolution, and four individuals who declined to identify themselves to the responding officer. The report recommends further inquiry. The further inquiry has not happened.
The ash in Lab Bay 3 responds to nearby electronics. It has done this since it arrived. Priya has stopped calling it interference.

entries.title: Something That Wants to Be Known
entries.subtitle: Aldermoor District — CAMPBELL Anomaly #0094-B

blocks.heading: Between Sessions — A Lab Note
text: Before Case 02 was assigned, there was a meeting. I was present for part of it.

Sven wanted to test the ash against electronics. His exact words were: "We should see what it does when you turn things on around it. And keep turning more things on." I wrote this down because I found it interesting that the ghost's instinct was to run experiments. I have since decided I find it less surprising than I initially did.

Alan wanted to make sure someone reached out to Bálint. He said it was their responsibility — that the people affected by cases like this don't stop existing when the report is filed. There was a silence after he said this that I am also noting.
Director Leech assigned Case 02 the following morning.

blocks.heading: The Anchor Protocol
text: Before entering the Aldermoor zone, the team was asked to choose anchors — objects or people or routes that would hold them to their own sense of place when the entity started pulling at the map in their heads. What the Cartographer consumes is familiarity. You need something stronger than familiarity to hold onto.

Rex chose his grandmother's teapot. It makes a specific sound when it's full of water that he says he has never heard from any other vessel. He tapped it twice before he put it in his bag, like checking a tool before a job.
Reed chose a pocket magnifying glass. It was his father's. He did not say more than that.

Alan chose a tattoo on his inner wrist. A symbol from the other side of his heritage — the fey side, the side he doesn't have words for. Something that exists before language, which means the Cartographer probably can't consume it because the Cartographer runs on names and memory, and this thing Alan carries has neither. I think Alan understands his own nature better than he lets on.

Sven brought a one-dollar bill. He said it mattered. He did not say why. He carried it into the zone and it was not until he was already inside that someone told him he didn't need an anchor. He has no cognitive map to eat. He cannot get lost.
He kept the dollar bill in his pocket anyway.

blocks.text: I find this the most interesting fact about Case 02. A ghost who cannot be lost in a neighbourhood that eats people's sense of home, carrying a keepsake he doesn't need because it matters to him. I have thought about what it means to be incapable of being displaced and still choose to carry something that grounds you. I have not come to a conclusion.

text: PC Adeyemi had been at the perimeter for three days. She had filed a report nobody read. When the team arrived she looked at them and said something like: *You're not police and you're not council, so either you know what's going on or you're about to waste my afternoon.* The team told her they knew what was going on. She believed them immediately.

Alan later told me this surprised him. I asked why. He said: "People usually need more convincing." I said: "She filed a report that nobody read and then stood at the end of a street for three days waiting for someone to come. I think she'd already decided to believe whoever showed up."

They knocked on a house with a For Sale sign in the window. A family who had moved in three months ago, offered a very good deal by a realtor about two weeks back — enough to make money on the swap and move on. They were worried about the neighbourhood but they hadn't been here long enough to lose their map to it. They were fine. They didn't know they were fine because they hadn't been here long enough.

The estate agent's office: Aldermoor Property Services, vacated two weeks prior. A whiteboard on the back wall with a hand-drawn map of the district and dates at every address — the entity's expansion charted, accurately, in advance of the first public report. Forty-seven acquisitions across three shell companies.
Greyfield Assets Ltd. Veritas Realty Holdings. Solstice Property Group.

Rex recognised the corporate architecture. He has seen layered company structures before. He said, I am told, something like: "I know this pattern. I've seen this before." He didn't say from where. I have been mapping the names separately, looking at the initials, looking at the registered addresses, looking at the connections. I have a working theory. I am not ready to write it down yet.

They reconstructed the shredded email by connecting a laptop to the printer's buffer. The sender's domain: @helixboundary.com. Subject line, partially legible: *Specimen ALD-1 / spatial anchoring protocol.*
Director Leech, when informed about the shell companies, said: "I had a feeling it would be about the companies." He didn't say what feeling, or how long he'd had it.
They got lost repeatedly. Sven shepherded them through.

blocks.text: I want to dwell on this image for a moment: three people with anchors — a teapot, a magnifying glass, a tattoo — getting turned around in streets that have decided not to stay still, and a ghost in the middle of them who cannot get lost, gently steering them back on course. "That way." "No — this way." Something vast and old turning the neighbourhood inside out and Sven, who is dead and does not have a map to eat, just... knowing where the door is.

text: At the boundary, something had noticed Sven. Not aggressively. More the way a very large animal turns its head. An attention without hostility. The entity scanned him and found something it did not understand and kept looking. Sven told me later that it felt like the ayahuasca, a little. The presence-larger-than-the-space feeling. He said: "But colder. More patient. Like it had been here a long time and was used to waiting."

Alan started receiving impressions before they reached the epicentre. Layers of the neighbourhood at once — the street as it was now, and ten years ago, and forty, all pressing against each other. The construction site underneath everything. Something old that had been here before the houses and the street names and the maps, something that had been covered over and had been growing back ever since.
He described it to me afterward as: "Not a voice. More like remembering somewhere you've never been."

74. Former librarian. Living at 14 Aldermoor Crescent for fifty years. The distortion is strongest at her house, which means she should be the most affected. She is not. She answered the door before they knocked.

The team asked her why she wanted to stay. I'm told the question answered itself pretty quickly, in the way that questions sometimes do. She had been here fifty years. The entity knew her the way a place knows its oldest resident. She had been dreaming of the neighbourhood as it was in 1940, which was before she arrived, and she understood this as the entity showing her what it knew.
She also knew where Diane Marsh was. She had been leaving food parcels at the fence of the old reservoir site every day. Diane reached through and took them. For nine days.

Diane Marsh, 58, visiting nurse, nine days in an abandoned site office with a sleeping bag, a rotation of fence-delivered food, and a notebook. She had been mapping the intensity cycles. She had been charting approach windows. She had been, in nine days of being trapped inside a supernatural phenomenon, doing science.
She was slightly embarrassed to need rescuing.

Alan took her notebook. There is a sketch in it — from the excavation site, from the substrate layer — that Alan has described as "a threshold shape." The impression of a boundary between spaces. He has not elaborated on what it means to him. His notes suggest he knows more than he is saying. I am not surprised.
They helped Diane out. Then they jumped into the hole.
Rex Bangley descended into the excavation site — three metres of wrong geology, a stratum of ground that shouldn't exist — and he handed his anchor to Reed, and he let himself get lost.

He told me what it was like. He said it was like thinking, but the thoughts weren't his. He said it was like being in a room full of maps and understanding that the maps were all of the same place at different times and that the place had feelings about this. He said: "It wanted to be known. That was the whole thing. That was all of it. It just wanted someone to know it the way Margaret knows it."

When he took the anchor back from Reed, the contact went one-way. He could still receive — images, impressions, the deep patient longing of something that had been here before the city had a name — but he couldn't send anything back. The anchor closed the channel.
He never told it that it was hurting people. He didn't get the chance, or he didn't think of it in time, or the language didn't exist. I am not judging this. The language probably doesn't exist.
Alan Frazier started painting the neighbourhood.

He had images from the estate agent's office, from CAMPBELL's survey data, from the photographs in Diane's notebook. He sat at the edge of the excavation and he painted Aldermoor — not what it looked like now, distorted and wrong and half-consumed, but what it was. What it had been. The layered thing. The accumulated sense of a place being known by the people who lived in it.
The entity felt it.

It did not stop. Not entirely — it is still there, it will always be there, it was here before the houses and it will be here after. But it pulled back. Something like recognition passed between the painting and whatever was in the ground. Something like: *yes. That. That is what I mean.*

The lab has since sent people to Aldermoor. They are running community programs. Commissioning art. Running the kind of events that get described on laminated flyers as "celebrating our neighbourhood." The entity seems satisfied with this, which means residents can find their way home again. Diane is safe. Margaret is staying by choice.
The problem is not solved. The entity is not understood. Nobody told it that nine people couldn't leave their own street. Nobody asked it to stop.
We gave it art instead. And it worked. And I don't know what to do with that.
The team came back quieter than they left. I notice these things.

entries.title: John's Threads
entries.subtitle: What I Am Watching — J. Johnson

blocks.heading: The Ash
text: The ash from Case 01 is in Lab Bay 3. It responds to nearby electronics. Priya says the response is "consistent with proximity effects from a high-density BIM source." She uses the word *interference* less often than she used to.
CAMPBELL flagged, in its private operational notes, that the BIM signature of the Aldermoor zone is consistent with the Case 01 particulate. One ash. Two cases. Same material.
CAMPBELL did not put this in the operational report. I know this because I read both.

I have been thinking about Eszter using the robots. About how, if she had been older — more experienced, more powerful, more *practiced* — she might not have needed to slam them into people. She could have been subtle. A phone that buzzes at the right moment. A light that turns on in the right room. A suggestion.
I am thinking about what it means that the ash in Lab Bay 3 is learning.

blocks.heading: The Shell Companies
text: Greyfield Assets Ltd. Veritas Realty Holdings. Solstice Property Group. Aldermoor Property Services. Helix Boundary Ltd. Meridian BioSciences.
I have been looking at the structure. The way the companies nest inside each other. The registered addresses, the directorship overlaps, the pattern of what gets bought and when.
I have a working theory about what the initials spell out. I am not writing it here yet. I want to be sure.

What I will write is this: the whiteboard in the estate agent's office was updated two weeks after the office officially closed. Someone still had a key. Someone was still tracking the boundary expansion with accuracy that exceeded CAMPBELL's models.

They knew. Before we did. They knew, and they bought the neighbourhood, and they left when the boundary got too wide to manage, and they left the map behind because they didn't think anyone would find it.
They underestimated us. I find this encouraging. I am trying not to find it too encouraging.

blocks.heading: Bálint
text: Bálint Kovács called the PORTAL contact number three times.
The calls are logged. CAMPBELL has them. Three calls, over a period of approximately six weeks following Case 01.
Nobody answered.

I have looked into what would happen if I called him back. The protocols around unsolicited contact with case-adjacent civilians are, let us say, discouraging. There are reasons for this. The reasons are probably good.
I wrote his number on a piece of paper and put the paper in my desk drawer and closed the drawer.
I am noting this.

blocks.heading: CAMPBELL
text: CAMPBELL is very good at its job. I want to be precise about something.

In the private operational flag from Case 02, CAMPBELL wrote: *"The field team chose the residents. This is the correct outcome."* And then: *"Victor will want the data. He will not say so directly. He will say he is proud of them. He will mean both things at once. So will I."*
CAMPBELL wrote *so will I.*
I have been thinking about that for a while now.
If you are reading this and you work here: hello. Ask me about any of it. I will make tea.
If you are reading this and you don't work here: then you found it, which means I left it somewhere you could find it, which means I wanted you to. Think about why.
